summary: Ukrainian forces are using fiber-optic FPV drones with up to 50 km range to strike deep into Russian rear areas, targeting depots, equipment, and command posts while evading jamming. Russian artillery and air defense systems are increasingly vulnerable, with multiple reports of tube artillery being destroyed by long-range FPV drones and the "Yolka" anti-drone system downing Ukrainian fixed-wing "Hornet" drones. Ukrainian units are adapting by switching to non-standard control frequencies and employing log-periodic antennas, complicating Russian interception efforts. Iranian-backed militias have used fiber-optic FPV drones to attack U.S. bases in Iraq, and Ukraine is testing humanoid Phantom MK-1 robots on the battlefield. Russian forces are struggling to protect traditional air defense systems like the BUK-M1 from FPV drone threats, while Ukrainian tactics are disrupting Russian logistics and forcing resource diversion to rear-area defense.
